data.title: Interactive Brokers signals continued global expansion and record client growth with 1M new accounts in 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Milan Galik, President and CEO, stated that "in 2025, we added more than 1 million net new accounts, an annual record for the firm." He highlighted that client equity rose 37% to $780 billion, marking the first time the company ended the year with over $0.75 trillion in client assets. Galik emphasized strong international interest and growing engagement in the global securities markets, particularly as interest rates decline and other financial institutions pay less.
  • Galik reported that clients outperformed the S&P 500 in 2025, noting, "individual investors here were up, on average, 19.2%... financial advisers were up 20.57% on average... hedge fund clients were up 28.91% on average." He attributed this to the company's focus on low pricing, superior trade execution, and advanced platforms.
  • The company introduced new products and platform enhancements, expanding market access to Brazil, Taiwan, the UAE, and Slovenia, and launched new tax-advantaged accounts in Sweden, Japan, and Canada. The GlobalTrader 2.0 mobile platform was launched with a redesigned interface and AI-powered tools such as "Ask IBKR" and AI-generated news summaries. Galik noted, "trading volume during our overnight hours continues to grow rapidly, up 76% from last quarter and more than 130% from the fourth quarter of last year."
  • ForecastEx, the company's prediction market exchange, "traded 286 million [pairs] this quarter, up from 15 million [pairs] in the third quarter and now has 4 members quoting into the exchange."
  • Paul Brody, CFO, stated, "commission revenues rose to a record $582 million this quarter. Net interest income reached $966 million for the quarter and a yearly record of $3.6 billion despite multiple rate cuts in nearly all major currencies."

Outlook

  • Management indicated ongoing global expansion, with additional countries planned for 2026 and further enhancements to platform features and tax-advantaged accounts. Galik projected, "we have many projects to work on, which we will look forward to sharing with you once they become a reality."
  • The bank charter application process is ongoing, with Galik stating, "my expectation would be to be operational by the end of this year" if approved. A European banking license is planned for the future, likely in Ireland, but is not considered urgent.
  • The company continues to invest in AI and technology-driven initiatives, anticipating expense growth in line with prior years, barring incremental growth from these initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Quarterly adjusted pretax income reached a record level of more than $1 billion for the fifth consecutive quarter. For the full year, net revenues surpassed $6 billion for the first time.
  • Brody highlighted that "execution, clearing and distribution costs were $91 million in the quarter, down 21% from the year ago quarter." Compensation and benefits expense was $153 million for the quarter. G&A expenses were $62 million, up 5% from the year ago quarter, driven primarily by advertising.
  • The firm's pretax margin matched the third quarter record 79% and achieved a new record 77% for the year. Firm equity exceeded $20 billion for the first time.
  • Options contract volumes were up 27% over the prior year quarter, futures contract volumes rose 22%, and stock share volumes increased 16%. Total customer DARTs were 4 million trades per day, up 30% from the prior year.

Q&A

  • Brennan Hawken, BMO Capital Markets: Asked about customer credit balances and the impact of lower rates. Paul Brody responded that "our very strong performance in the margin lending balances overcame -- vastly overcame the drop in the general benchmark rates."
  • Hawken followed up on the bank charter. Galik explained, "my expectation would be to be operational by the end of this year." The charter would enable IBKR to custody assets of mutual funds and ETFs.
  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler: Inquired about prediction markets and regulatory developments. Thomas Peterffy commented, "your guess is as good as ours as to what will happen here. Luckily, Interactive Brokers does not rely on sports."
  • Moley asked about M&A and capital return priorities. Peterffy stated, "we're not going to buy a firm that is doing sports betting," and Galik added, "ForecastEx...is growing nicely. No reason for us to look for an acquisition in this space."
  • James Yaro, Goldman Sachs: Asked about a European banking license. Galik replied, "we have not started the process. It is not urgent for us to have one. We will eventually have a license in Europe."
  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: Sought insights on crypto adoption. Galik answered, "crypto revenues are at the moment, small relative to the overall company's revenues...Our offering is competitive, and we continue to add capabilities. New geographies, namely Europe is currently the focus."
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Inquired about expense growth and account growth trends. Galik said, "overall expense growth...has been consistent...about 6% growth" in headcount, expecting similar growth in the future. Account growth remains strong across all client segments and geographies.
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: Asked about advertising and prediction markets. Peterffy responded, "advertising is certainly a key. We are doing -- getting better and better at advertising and we are also increasing our spending."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed curiosity and cautious optimism, probing for details on bank charter progress, prediction markets, crypto expansion, and expense growth. Tone was neutral to slightly positive, with occasional pressing for more detail, particularly on regulatory matters and growth prospects.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone, emphasizing growth and operational milestones. Statements such as "I don't see any reason why our account growth would slow down" (Thomas Peterffy) and "we have many projects to work on" (Galik) reflected optimism. Tone remained consistent with the previous quarter, with confidence in core growth drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company added a record 1 million net new accounts in 2025, up from 790,000 through the third quarter. Client equity increased from surpassing $0.75 trillion in Q3 to $780 billion by year-end.
  • Global expansion continued, with market entry into four new countries in Q4 and additional country-specific tax-advantaged products launched.
  • Platform enhancements accelerated, including the launch of GlobalTrader 2.0 and AI-powered tools. ForecastEx trading volume rose sharply from 15 million pairs in Q3 to 286 million pairs in Q4.
  • Financial performance set new records in pretax income and net revenues. Headcount and expense growth remained consistent with prior quarters.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from regulatory environment and product mix in previous quarters to regulatory approvals (bank charter), prediction markets, and global product rollouts in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged uncertainty around regulatory outcomes for prediction markets, stating, "your guess is as good as ours as to what will happen here."
  • The company remains exposed to interest rate fluctuations, estimating a $77 million reduction in annual net interest income for a 25 basis point decrease in the Fed funds rate.
  • Crypto revenues are still small, with slow adoption among some client segments. Management is monitoring asset transfers and new geographies for expansion.
  • Expense growth, particularly related to AI initiatives, may rise, though management continues to emphasize discipline.

Final Takeaway

Interactive Brokers concluded 2025 with record-breaking growth in accounts, client equity, and financial results, driven by global expansion, platform innovation, and a broadening product suite. Management remains optimistic about continued account growth, technology-driven enhancements, and further market expansion, while maintaining focus on operational discipline and readiness to address regulatory developments and shifting market dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cenovus considers selling ~C$3B in Alberta oil and gas assets - Reuters
data.description:
Cenovus Energy (CVE) is considering a sale of conventional oil and gas assets in the Deep Basin of Alberta, Reuters reported late Tuesday, as it seeks to reduce debt after the recent takeover of oil sands rival MEG Energy.

Cenovus (CVE) has reached out to potential buyers in recent weeks to gauge interest in the assets, which could fetch ~C$3B (US$2.17B), according to the report, which cautioned the plans are at an early stage, and the company ultimately could decide to retain the assets.

Cenovus' (CVE) potential sale of Deep Basin assets come as it sharpens focus on its core oil sands business after completing a C$8.5B deal for MEG Energy in November, which added the smaller company's coveted Christina Lake project to its portfolio.

A sale also would help Cenovus (CVE) clean up its balance sheet, as its net debt jumped to ~C$10.7B after the MEG Energy takeover, when it assumed C$800M of MEG's debt and took out a C$2.7B loan to fund the deal, according to Morningstar DBRS estimates.

data.title: Oil prices rise on Kazakhstan production halt, healthy Chinese GDP growth
data.description:
Crude oil futures gained Tuesday following reports of a production halt in Kazakhstan's major Tengiz oilfield due to power outages, and U.S. natural gas futures surged by the most in nearly four years in anticipation of a bitter cold stretch in the northeastern U.S.

Kazakh oil producer Tengizchevroil, led by Chevron (CVX), said Monday it temporarily halted production at the Tengiz and Korolev oilfields after an issue affected power distribution systems, and Reuters cited industry sources as saying output at Tengiz could be halted for 7-10 days.

Kazakhstan already had reduced oil production after drone strikes affecting the Caspian Pipeline Consortium's shipping terminal in Russia, which is the outlet for ~80% of Kazakh exports.

"Tengiz is among the largest fields in the world, so the outage is certainly disruptive for crude flows," ICIS director of energy and refining Ajay Parmar said in a note, but "this disruption does look to be temporary, and so if the tariffs rhetoric continues, we expect prices to fall back."

The oil market also was supported by better than expected gross domestic product data from China, which said Monday its economy grew 5% in 2025 and refinery throughput climbed 4.1% Y/Y.

China's GDP data "reinforced the view that activity in the world's largest crude importer is stabilizing, offering a constructive signal for demand expectations," Naga's Frank Walbaum said in a note, however, "the prospect of a wider U.S.-Europe trade confrontation has injected caution into the outlook, given the potential drag on global growth and, by extension, oil demand."

Fears of a renewed trade war escalated over the weekend after President Trump said he would impose additional 10% tariffs on goods imported from eight European countries, rising to 25% on June 1 if no deal on Greenland was reached.

Trump's tariff threats are negative for crude prices, as their implementation could lead to lower global economic growth and reduced oil demand growth, Parmar said.

Both major crude oil benchmarks closed +1.5%, with front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for February delivery ending at $60.34/bbl and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for March delivery finishing at $64.92/bbl.

U.S. natgas futures soared as forecasts turned much colder over the long holiday weekend, with a deep freeze expected to grip much of the country during the coming weeks.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for February delivery settled +25.9% to $3.907/MMBtu, its largest one-day dollar gain since September 14, 2022, and biggest one-day percentage gain since January 27, 2022.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Progress Software targets $1B revenue for FY 2026 as AI-driven demand fuels growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Progress Software Corporation (PRGS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Yogesh Gupta stated that fiscal year 2025 was Progress' strongest year-to-date, highlighting that "fiscal year '25 was Progress' strongest year-to-date, driven by a combination of ShareFile and the strong performance of our overall product portfolio, especially during the second half of the year, which was increasingly propelled by our customers' AI projects." He reported annual revenue of $978 million, a 30% increase year-over-year, and earnings per share of $5.72, up 16% from fiscal year 2024.

  • Gupta announced: "For the fourth quarter, revenue finished at $253 million, up 18% year-over-year, right in line with our most recent guideline. Earnings of $1.51 were well above the high end of guidance, thanks yet again to excellent expense discipline and consistent execution."

  • Gupta highlighted successful integration of ShareFile, stating, "We passed every milestone and met every goal on or ahead of schedule." Additionally, Nuclia's agentic RAG technology has been fully integrated and is "adding significant functionality and value to our products."

  • He described recent product innovations: "We launched Progress agentic RAG... introduced the industry's first generative content management system with built-in RAG capabilities in Sitefinity... launched an enterprise-grade agentic UI generator... and Automate MFT, a new cloud-native file transfer solution."

  • CFO Anthony Folger said, "We closed Q4 with ARR of $852 million, approximately 2% pro forma year-over-year growth. For clarity, our pro forma results include ARR from acquired businesses in all periods presented." He added, "Q4 revenue was $253 million, up approximately 18% year-over-year. Our revenue strength in the quarter was broad-based with outperformance coming from OpenEdge and ShareFile."

Outlook

  • Gupta indicated, "Looking ahead, the high end of our initial guidance for FY '26 is $1 billion, a very exciting milestone for Progress with unlevered free cash flow of nearly $320 million at the midpoint."

  • Folger provided, "For the first quarter of 2026, we expect revenue between $244 million and $250 million and earnings per share of between $1.56 and $1.62. For the full year 2026, we expect revenue between $986 million and $1 billion, representing between 1% and 2% growth over 2025, an operating margin of 39%, adjusted free cash flow between $260 million and $274 million and unlevered free cash flow between $313 million and $326 million."

  • Folger reiterated, "We will continue to focus on ARR as a key metric and we expect ARR growth generally consistent with the 2% growth we saw in fiscal year 2025."

Financial Results

  • The company reported ARR of $852 million, with a net retention rate of 100%.

  • Q4 revenue was $253 million, an 18% increase year-over-year. Full year revenue reached $978 million, up $224 million from the prior year, attributed to the full year contribution from ShareFile.

  • Total costs and operating expenses were $156 million for the quarter and $593 million for the full year, with the increase driven by ShareFile.

  • Operating income for the quarter was $96 million, with an operating margin of 38%. Earnings per share was $1.51, $0.16 above the high end of guidance.

  • Cash and cash equivalents stood at $95 million, with debt of $1.4 billion and a net leverage ratio of approximately 3.4x. Deferred revenue at quarter end was $425 million, up $21 million year-over-year. Adjusted free cash flow was $62 million for the quarter and $247 million for the year, up 16%. Share repurchases totaled $40 million in Q4 and $105 million for the year.

Q&A

  • John DiFucci, Guggenheim Securities: Asked about the evolution of AI and its impact on software, particularly for Progress. Gupta responded that while there is "a level of hype... that has led to the level of fear around the disruption of software," he does not see a near-term shift, stating, "in the near term, in my mind, is next 1 to 3 years, are, to be honest, way overblown."

  • Fatima Boolani, Citi: Inquired about visibility of AI monetization in net retention rates. Gupta noted, "even today, vast majority of investment in AI is limited... a relatively small number of tech companies," describing it as early days for broader monetization.

  • Fatima Boolani, Citi: Asked about free cash flow seasonality for 2026. Folger explained, "Q4 was a great quarter in terms of cash flow. Q4 was also a quarter where we had a significant beat on bookings... I wouldn't expect material differences in the seasonality or the linearity of our free cash flow from where we've been historically."

  • Lucky Schreiner, D.A. Davidson: Questioned SaaS revenue growth and its outlook. Folger responded, "Q4 was a really strong quarter on the SaaS line... ShareFile is growing well, but it's a single-digit grower."

  • Schreiner also asked about retention rates at acquisition targets. Gupta acknowledged, "there is a bit more of a turn... so yes, we are seeing softening in their both gross and net retention rates."

  • Nolan Jenevein, Oppenheimer: Asked about operating margin assumptions for next year. Folger detailed, "we were able to integrate ShareFile more quickly and at a much lower cost than we expected... as we look out into '26, we're already at our target margin."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive yet probing tone, with questions focused on AI's impact, monetization, and margin sustainability. There was mild skepticism regarding the pace of AI-driven benefits translating into financial metrics, particularly net retention and SaaS growth.

  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, with phrases like "we are thrilled" and "excited about the year ahead." In Q&A, Gupta was measured and rational, especially addressing AI disruption concerns and acquisition selectivity, while Folger highlighted disciplined execution and cost control.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remains positive but slightly more cautious around AI monetization timing and the durability of recent margin gains. Management's tone is consistently confident, while analysts' tone is constructive but attentive to risks and the pace of change.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue and EPS for Q4 increased over the prior quarter, with full-year revenue and earnings per share both showing double-digit percentage growth.

  • Guidance for FY 2026 is for modest revenue and ARR growth, consistent with the current year, reflecting a more tempered outlook versus prior quarters' larger jumps.

  • ShareFile's integration and contribution were again highlighted as key drivers, with new AI products and capabilities rolled out during Q4.

  • Management's tone remains optimistic, pointing to momentum and disciplined M&A strategy, while analysts continue to focus on the sustainability of AI-driven growth and margin improvements.

Risks and Concerns

  • Gupta acknowledged that "few such assets have come to market" in M&A targets, indicating a challenging acquisition environment and the need for continued selectivity.

  • Gupta noted that "there is a bit more of a turn" and "softening in their both gross and net retention rates" at some acquisition targets, reflecting risks in smaller software companies facing AI-driven competitive pressures.

  • Folger cited a net leverage ratio of 3.4x, with plans for aggressive debt repayment, signaling ongoing attention to balance sheet strength.

Final Takeaway

Progress Software ended fiscal 2025 with its strongest performance to date, driven by ShareFile integration and expanding AI-driven demand across its product portfolio. Management projects achieving up to $1 billion in revenue in 2026, targeting stable growth and continued innovation in AI capabilities, while maintaining a disciplined approach to M&A and cost control. Although the environment for acquisitions and AI monetization remains competitive and gradual, the company expresses confidence in its growth trajectory, operating leverage, and ability to deliver shareholder value in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ServisFirst signals continued margin expansion and targets robust Texas growth in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ServisFirst Bancshares (SFBS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Broughton indicated that loan growth for the quarter was in line with projections, reporting "annualized growth of 12% for the quarter." He noted pipeline increases and a diminishing payoff headwind, stating "the payoff headwind is diminishing to some extent" and expressed optimism for further improvement. Broughton highlighted ongoing management of high-cost municipal deposits and a strong deposit-gathering position, remarking that robust loan demand would allow the bank to "attract some of those type deposits back if they are needed."
  • Broughton announced the launch of a new Texas banking team based in Houston, describing the group as "very excited" and "productive in temporary office space already," with a current team of nine and more hires expected in the first two quarters of 2026. He emphasized that "this is a much larger team than we have hired in the recent past" and expressed high expectations for the region.
  • The company expanded its correspondent banking footprint, reaching 388 banks and growing its agent credit card program to 150 banks, with further endorsements from new state banking associations.
  • CFO David Sparacio stated, "we recorded $1.58 of earnings per diluted share for the fourth quarter, which is a 32% increase from the third quarter of 2025 and a 33% increase from the fourth quarter of 2024." Sparacio added, "net income available to common shareholders was $86.4 million for the quarter and $276.5 million for the year."
  • Sparacio reported that "our net interest margin experienced healthy growth throughout 2025, rising from 2.92% in the first quarter to 3.38% in the fourth quarter." He attributed the expansion to disciplined loan pricing and deposit rate reductions.
  • COO Rodney Rushing highlighted a "4%" increase in tangible book value per share to $33.62 during the quarter.

Outlook

  • Management expects the Texas team to drive significant growth in 2026, with Broughton stating, "we have great expectations from Texas" and that the group's "budgeted growth for 2026 is higher than any other region."
  • On margin, Sparacio told analysts, "using the December spot margin is a good starting point for 2026," and forecasted further expansion, supported by loan repricing: "we expect continued margin expansion...throughout 2026."
  • Regarding expense growth, Sparacio said, "we're thinking high single digit" for 2026, noting the impact of new hires but expecting efficiency ratio to remain in the low 30s.
  • Tax strategy will focus on maximizing credits, particularly "around solar credits," with Broughton stating, "we're going to continue to try to manage that down going forward."

Financial Results

  • Net interest margin rose to "3.38% in the fourth quarter," up from "2.92% in the first quarter."
  • Loan growth was "equally split between our C&I and real estate portfolios with about 10% annual growth in each."
  • Noninterest revenue increased with a "26% growth from full year 2024 to full year 2025" in service charges and an "11% annual increase in mortgage banking fee income."
  • Efficiency ratio fell below "30% for the quarter," with the full-year adjusted ratio at "near 32%, which is a 14% improvement over 2024."
  • Noninterest expense was "flat and down about 3% versus linked quarters," and up "only 2%" for the full year.
  • The company paid down "$30 million of sub debt" and increased its dividend.
  • Allowance for credit losses was "1.25%" at year-end.

Q&A

  • David Bishop, Hovde Group: Asked about payoff trends and loan demand. Broughton responded, "projected payoffs has dropped substantially quarter-over-quarter...at least it's trending in the right direction."
  • Bishop followed up on Texas team expectations. Broughton replied, "their budgeted growth for 2026 is higher than any other region...they're primarily C&I lenders."
  • Steve Moss, Raymond James: Inquired about margin sustainability and repricing opportunities. Sparacio answered, "using the December spot margin is a good starting point for 2026," and detailed "about $2 billion opportunity" for repricing loans in the next 12 months.
  • Moss also asked about the $5 million charge-off and multifamily nonperforming assets. Broughton confirmed, "the charge was related to the health care asset...largely has been put behind us now," while the multifamily issue is being worked through with the borrower.
  • Moss queried about expense growth, with Sparacio stating, "we're thinking high single digit" and emphasizing producer hires rather than back-office staff.
  • Moss asked about M&A disruptions and hiring. Broughton said, "we're going to hire as many good people as we can find."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts asked probing questions about payoff trends, loan demand, margin sustainability, credit quality, and Texas expansion, maintaining a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking clarity but not expressing major concern.
  • Management's tone was confident during prepared remarks, particularly about margin expansion and Texas growth, and remained consistent during Q&A, providing detailed responses and reiterating optimism for 2026. Broughton stated, "we are pleased...with a good ending" to 2025 and expressed excitement for 2026.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted from cautious—focused on loan paydowns and nonperformers—to more constructive, while management's confidence increased, especially regarding margin trajectory and strategic hires.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism on loan growth and margin in Q3 to more assertive targets for margin expansion and Texas-led growth in Q4.
  • Management focus moved from managing loan paydowns and addressing nonperformers in Q3 to executing on new market expansion and booking robust loan growth in Q4.
  • Analysts pivoted from concerns about credit quality and loan pipeline in Q3 to margin sustainability and the impact of new hires in Q4.
  • Key metrics improved: net interest margin expanded, efficiency ratio declined, and loan growth rebounded versus prior quarter.
  • Management expressed greater confidence in cost controls and organic growth, expecting new initiatives to drive performance in 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Credit quality risk was noted, with nonperforming assets rising to "97 basis points" of total assets, attributed to a single merchant developer exposure.
  • Management is actively managing problem loans, with Harper stating, "we continue to proactively manage our loan portfolio achieving a number of successful outcomes."
  • Expense growth risk from Texas expansion was acknowledged, with Sparacio noting the expectation for higher expenses but neutralizing impact with revenue growth.
  • Multifamily portfolio remains under close watch, as management works with borrowers for orderly resolution.

Final Takeaway

ServisFirst Bancshares concluded 2025 with strong momentum, highlighted by robust loan growth, expanding net interest margins, and disciplined cost control. With the addition of a new Texas team positioned for outsized growth in 2026 and a pipeline of repricing opportunities expected to support margin expansion, management expressed optimism for continued performance gains. Credit quality remains a focus, but proactive management and balanced portfolio growth strategies underpin a confident outlook as the company advances into the new year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: United Airlines rallies after saying it's on track for record profits
data.description:
United Airlines (UAL) rallied in postmarket trading on Tuesday after the airline company said it may churn up record earnings this year. The company's confident forecast was based on strong travel demand trends. Notably, United (UAL) said business bookings were up double digits over the last two weeks

For the full year, United (YUAL) sees EPS of $12 and $14 (midpoint $13.00) vs. the consensus estimate of $13.19. 

As part of its update, United (UAL) noted that it plans to enhance the customer experience as it plans to take delivery of over 100 narrowbody aircraft and approximately 20 Boeing 787 aircraft, which would be more widebody aircraft in a year than any U.S. passenger airline has had taken in since 1988. United (UAL) plans to use the new aircraft to profitably expand its network, including its industry-leading international routes and growing domestic network. In addition, the airline aims to make significant airport upgrades at its Washington Dulles and Houston hubs.

Shares of UAL rose 3.3% in postmarket trading.

data.title: Petrobras signs $521M in contracts for five gas carriers, other vessels
data.description:
Petrobras (PBR) signed contracts with shipyards for five gas carriers, 18 barges and 18 pushers for a combined 2.8B reais (~$521M), Reuters reported Tuesday.

A shipyard in Rio Grande do Sul will build the five tankers, representing 2.2B reais of the deal, to carry liquefied petroleum gas and derivatives, according to the report.

Two of the tankers will have a capacity of 14K cm, and the other three will be able to transport 7K cm each, with the first gas tanker scheduled for delivery within 33 months after construction begins and new deliveries every six months, the company said.

"With these contracts, we are preparing Petrobras for the growth of our production in the coming years and boosting the recovery of the national shipbuilding industry," Petrobras (PBR) CEO Magda Chambriard said.

Separately, Petrobras (PBR) said Friday it produced 2.4M bbl/day of oil and total oil and gas production reached 2.99M boe/day in FY 2025, both up 11% from 2024 and above the upper limit of the targets set by the company in its 2025-29 business plan.

data.title: Rio Tinto reports higher Q4 copper output as huge Mongolian mine ramps up
data.description:
Rio Tinto (RIO) exceeded expectations for quarterly iron ore and copper production, reporting Wednesday that iron ore production at its core Pilbara operations hit a record and copper output increased due to the continued expansion of its operation at Oyu Tolgoi in Mongolia.

Q4 shipments of iron ore, still Rio’s (RIO) biggest source of revenue by far, jumped 7% Y/Y to 91.3M metric tons, above the Visible Alpha consensus estimate of 88.2M tons, while output rose by 4% to 89.7M tons, but full-year exports of 326.2M tons fell 1% and came in near the lower end of its forecast range of 323M-338M tons, "with a strong recovery from the extreme weather interruptions earlier in the year."

The company said mined copper production on a consolidated basis rose 5% Y/Y to 240K metric tons, above the Visible Alpha consensus estimate of 214.4K tons, while annual output climbed 11% to 883K tons, topping its guidance of 860K-875K tons, driven by the continued underground ramp-up, higher grades and recovery rates at the Oyu Tolgoi mine.

Rio Tinto (RIO) disclosed earlier this month it is in talks with Glencore about a potential deal that could create the world's largest mining company, but the negotiations were not discussed in its quarterly update.

data.title: Energy Fuels to buy Australian Strategic Materials in $299M deal
data.description:
Energy Fuels (UUUU) +1.1% post-market Tuesday after saying it agreed to acquire rare earth metals producer Australian Strategic Materials (ASMMF) for US$299M, to be completed through a scheme of arrangement under Australian law.

Energy Fuels (UUUU) said it is creating what it believes will be the largest, fully integrated rare earth elements "mine-to-metal and alloy" producer outside of China to close a critical strategic gap in global supply chains for magnet applications.

The transaction will combine Australian Strategic Materials' operating Korean Metals Plant - one of the few facilities outside of China currently producing REE metals and alloys - and its planned American Metals Plant with Energy Fuels' (UUUU) existing REE oxide production at its White Mesa Mill in Utah, the only U.S. facility capable of separating monazite concentrates into both light and heavy REE oxides that are planned to be utilized in ASM's metallization and alloying facilities in South Korea and the U.S.

Energy Fuels (UUUU) rose more than 7% in regular trading as B. Riley raised its price target to $27 with a Buy rating on the shares.
